title: Westerlund and Narayan predictability test: Step-by-step approach using COVID-19 and oil price data
author: Sharma
description: In this note, we provide a step-by-step approach of Westerlund and Narayan (WN, 2012, 2015) predictability test using COVID-19 and oil price data. This is an important exercise because the WN model addresses three salient features of time series data, namely persistency, endogeneity and heteroskedasticity. We consider COVID-19 and oil price data as predictors of stock market returns for four Asian countries to demonstrate the applicability of the WN (2012, 2015) predictability approach.•This note demonstrates a step-by-step approach of the WN (2012, 2015) predictability test.•WN model accommodates three salient features of time-series data, namely persistency, endogeneity, and heteroskedasticity.•COVID-19 and oil price does not significantly predict stock returns of Japan, Russia, and Singapore (except in the case of South Korea).

steps_raw.title: Step-by-Step method
steps_raw.instruction: The following steps are followed in order to examine the time-series predictability using the WN approach.

steps_raw.title: Step-by-Step method
steps_raw.instruction: Step 1: Implement a predictive regression model, where the dependent variable (denoted by Yt) is regressed on the one-period lagged (or past information) predictor variable (denoted by Xt−1), which can be written as follows:(1)Yt=∝+βXt−1+eY,t

steps_raw.title: Step-by-Step method
steps_raw.instruction: Step 2: It is possible that in step 1, the predictor variable is endogenous. If it is, it needs to be controlled by estimating the following regression:(2)Xt=μ(1−γ)+γXt−1+eX,tWhere eX,t is mean zero and with variance σX2.

steps_raw.title: Step-by-Step method
steps_raw.instruction: Step 3: Test for endogeneity by extracting residuals from Eqs. (1) and (2). We assume that the error terms are linearly related and can be estimated in the following way:(3)eY,t=θeX,t+εt

steps_raw.title: Step-by-Step method
steps_raw.instruction: Here, we are testing the null hypothesis of no endogeneity by setting θ=0 against an alternative that θ≠0.

steps_raw.title: Step-by-Step method
steps_raw.instruction: Step 4: Estimate the final WN model, which uses two estimators, bias-adjusted ordinary least squares (OLS) and the generalized least squares (GLS). Both these estimators are based on Eq. (1) which is conditional on Eq. (2). This process removes the effect of the endogeneity through accommodating θ and persistency is accounted for by accommodating γ; therefore, the resulting conditional predictive regression model that is finally estimated is as follows:(4)Yt=∝−θμ(1−γ)+βadjXt−1+θΔXt+εt

steps_raw.title: Step-by-Step method
steps_raw.instruction: Here, we show that by construction εt is independent of eX,t and βadj=β−θ(γ−1).

steps_raw.title: Step-by-Step method
steps_raw.instruction: Step 5: Control for heteroskedasticity by using the GLS estimator instead of using the OLS estimator. Therefore, it is assumed that εt has the following autoregressive conditional heteroskedastic (ARCH) structure:(5)στ,t2=ωt+∑i=1pωiεt−i2

steps_raw.title: Step-by-Step method
steps_raw.instruction: WN (2015) show that the conditional variance of εt in Eq. (5) is var(εtIt−1)=σε,t2=θ2σε,t2=στ,t2. Thus, the GLS t-statistics will have the following form:(6)tGLS=β^GLS1/∑i=2Tφj2(Xt−1b)2

steps_raw.title: Step-by-Step method
steps_raw.instruction: Here, β^GLS is the GLS estimator of β from Eq. (5), φj=1/σY,t is the GLS weight, and Xtb=Xt−∑k=2TXk/T, where T is the sample size. Hence, this can be simply interpreted as: the test statistic is dependent on endogeneity and persistency, which is the estimate of beta whereas heteroskedasticity appears in the denominator (which is the inverse of the standard deviation of adjusted dependent variable).

steps_raw.title: Step-by-Step method
steps_raw.instruction: Step 6: Obtain βadj from Eq. (4), which denotes the coefficient of predictor variable.

steps_raw.title: Step-by-Step method
steps_raw.instruction: Step 7: Test the null hypothesis of no predictability by setting βadj=0 against the alternative that βadj≠0.
